title: The Theme of Failure in Evangelion
display_title: The Theme of Failure in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Theme of Failure in Evangelion             Quote Post by GAP » Wed May 02, 2012 9:36 pm You know I read a post on Venture Bros. and Evangelion and how they are similar with their deconstruction and character study but does Evangelion utilize the similar theme of failure?

[]:             Quote Post by someguy » Wed May 02, 2012 10:09 pm Nope, Asuka utterly falls apart into an infantile state and ends up lying in a bathtub full of her own blood because she pretty much always got everything right. Also, the animation in the last four episodes was a failure, amIrite? *rimshot*

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed May 02, 2012 10:25 pm This thread sure is not as interesting as the title lead me to believe it might be.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu May 03, 2012 1:10 pm Perhaps the OP can elaborate a bit more. The "theme of failure" aspect sounds interesting, but most of the users won't get much from it via a casual comparison to Venture Bros.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu May 03, 2012 2:27 pm I can see how that could apply in terms of the inevitable 3rd impact... is it an unavoidable fail condition and if so is it best to accept and even speed it along or to fight against it even knowing all that is likely to be accomplished is prolonging and deepening the suffering of the ordeal? Perhaps it's best to help it along and guide it to the least bad option (though disagreement about what this is is expected)? Is accepting 3I as inivitable a failure in and of itself? Is EoE's version of events a failure, a triumph, or a Pyrrhic victory? What about EoTV's ending? Is it a greater failure to not win or to not try?

[]:             Quote Post by someguy » Thu May 03, 2012 2:47 pm Well to be more serious failure is a huge theme throughout the entire series and EoE (part of Anno's depression?), but the OP is very vague.

[]:             Quote Post by Henry Spencer » Thu May 03, 2012 5:46 pm someguy wrote: Nope, Asuka utterly falls apart into an infantile state and ends up lying in a bathtub full of her own blood because she pretty much always got everything right. Also, the animation in the last four episodes was a failure, amIrite? *rimshot*

[]:             Quote Post by someguy » Thu May 03, 2012 7:56 pm Henry Spencer wrote: In episodes 23 and 24? Seriously?

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Thu May 03, 2012 8:48 pm I'll try elaborate (or attempt to at least to) on what I was trying to ask. Venture Bros. is an action adventure comedy about Hank and Dean Venture, their father Dr. Thaddues "Rusty" Venture and their bodyguard Brock Samson going on misadventures while fighting off villains like the Monarch. It seems at first like a Johnny Quest clone but it is actually parody that deconstructs the very genre it is paroding and shwoing what wrecks they would if they were in the real world. The theme of the series is failure, it is not that the heroes and/or villains did not try but rather their expectations did match up to the real world. The idealism has been ripped out of cast and as a result they had been reduced to psychotic wrecks. Wikipedia explains better than me: In the commentary for the episode "Home Insecurity", Hammer and Publick elaborated on the theme. Publick: "This show... If you'll permit me to get 'big picture', this show is actually all about failure. Even in the design, everything is supposed to be kinda the death of the space-age dream world. The death of the jet-age promises." Hammer: "It's about the beauty of failure. It's about that failure happens to all of us...Every character is not only flawed, but sucks at what they do, and is beautiful at it and Jackson and I suck at what we do, and we try to be beautiful at it, and failure is how you get by...It shows that failure's funny, and it's beautiful and it's life, and it's okay, and it's all we can write because we are big...failures. (laughter)"

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu May 03, 2012 9:23 pm Ah. In that case, the answer is no. Evangelion doesn't celebrate brokeness and failure, it confronts it, traces it back to the root and demands we answer why we keep letting this shit hold us back.

[]:             Quote Post by someguy » Thu May 03, 2012 11:40 pm I would say that a theme is that not everyone can be perfect, and it is human to make mistakes, so it is similar in that sense... Shinji is always a mess, and Asuka becomes a mess (as if she was ever normal), because of their fear of failure, but a point of the story is that everyone fails at some point but to accept that and move on instead of dwelling on it (or celebrating it). Rei feails a reasonable amount given her age and the circumstances but soldiers on regardless. Misato (at least in her work) and Gendo never even consider the possibility of failure, and as a result their every strategy is either a success or they turn it into one. Well, ignoring the end of EoE, but everyone gets kind of reamed there.

[]:             Quote Post by sum_exsanguis » Fri May 04, 2012 9:14 am as corny as it is couldn't you say that the theme is we musn't run away from the possibility of failure?

[]:             Quote Post by KimochiWarui » Fri May 04, 2012 9:16 am I think it more confronts failure than embraces it. Well that, or you could always just turn into delicious tang. Mmm.

[]:             Quote Post by someguy » Wed May 02, 2012 10:09 pm Nope, Asuka utterly falls apart into an i
